items.q: Which words are capitalised in German?
items.correct: All nouns (and the first word of a sentence).
items.misconception: Only proper nouns, as in English.
items.random_wrong: Only the first word of a sentence.
items.why: Students apply English rules. German capitalises EVERY noun (Haus, Liebe, Auto, Bewegung). It's a strict rule and a marker of accurate writing - lowercase nouns instantly look wrong to natives.

items.q: How do you say 'I drove to Munich' in the perfect tense?
items.why: Students use haben. Verbs of MOTION (fahren, gehen, kommen, fliegen, reisen) and CHANGE OF STATE take SEIN in the perfect. Critical for holiday vocab. 'Ich bin gegangen', 'ich bin geflogen', 'ich bin gefahren'.
Students assume gender follows meaning. German gender is grammatical, not biological. The diminutive suffix -CHEN makes any noun neuter, regardless of natural gender. So das Mädchen, das Mädel.

items.q: Which is the Präteritum of 'kommen' (3rd person sg)?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: kam. “kommt” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: When is Tag der Deutschen Einheit?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 3 October. “1 May” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Germany reunified?
items.why: The real figure is 1990. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
